topics.title: Human-Centered Design
topics.summary: Design should begin with human needs, capabilities, and behavior rather than forcing people to adapt to the system.
topics.fact: This approach puts human needs, capabilities, and behavior first when designing interactive systems.
topics.example: Norman's definition of human-centered design

topics.title: Interaction Sequence
topics.summary: Interactive systems involve repeated cycles of human input and system output over a sequence of actions.
topics.fact: The lecture used examples like a microwave to show how input and output alternate during use.
topics.example: Entering time, pressing start, and seeing countdown feedback

topics.title: Human-Centered Design Process
topics.summary: The design process connects understanding people, design, engineering, and evaluation through iteration.
topics.fact: The process was shown as involving user research, design, engineering, understanding people, and evaluation.
topics.example: Iterating between understanding people and evaluation

topics.title: HCI as an Interdisciplinary Field
topics.summary: HCI combines technical, design, and behavioral perspectives rather than belonging to a single discipline.
topics.fact: The lecture framed HCI with a three-legged stool analogy.
topics.example: Computer science, design, and behavioral sciences

topics.title: Why Human-Centered Design Matters
topics.summary: Focusing on users leads to safer, more usable, and more commercially successful systems.
topics.fact: The lecture tied HCI to widespread technology use, safety, and product acceptance.
topics.example: Usability issues causing a car recall

topics.title: Common Design Mistakes
topics.summary: Poor design often comes from optimizing for graphics, ideal conditions, or developer assumptions instead of real use.
topics.fact: One mistake discussed was designing only for ideal use and context.
topics.example: Ignoring real-world use conditions

topics.title: Components of Usability
topics.summary: Usability includes learnability, memorability, efficiency, low error rates, and satisfaction rather than being one single property.
topics.fact: Nielsen's framing treats usability as a multi-dimensional quality of interfaces.
topics.example: Easy to learn, easy to remember, efficient, few errors, satisfying

topics.title: Learnability and Efficiency Tradeoff
topics.summary: Designs that help novice users can differ from designs that help expert users work quickly.
topics.fact: The lecture compared easy-to-learn systems with systems optimized for efficient expert use.
topics.example: Accelerators, templates, and defaults for expert users

topics.title: ISO Usability Definition
topics.summary: Usability is defined in terms of effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction for specified users performing specified tasks.
topics.fact: The lecture emphasized both the user and the task in the ISO definition.
topics.example: Specified users completing specified tasks

topics.title: Seven Stages of Action and Gulfs
topics.summary: Norman's action cycle highlights the gulf of execution and gulf of evaluation as barriers between goals, actions, and interpretation.
topics.fact: The gulf of execution concerns translating goals into actions, while the gulf of evaluation concerns judging results.
topics.example: Users not knowing what to do next or whether an action worked

topics.title: Affordances, Signifiers, Mapping, and Feedback
topics.summary: Good interfaces make actions discoverable through affordances, clear signifiers, sensible mapping, and understandable feedback.
topics.fact: The lecture treated signifiers as clues that communicate available affordances.
topics.example: A labeled control that clearly indicates what action it performs

topics.title: User Experience Beyond Usability
topics.summary: User experience includes feelings, pleasure, and emotional response in addition to task success and efficiency.
topics.fact: The lecture connected UX to broader emotional design ideas from the 2000s.
topics.example: Norman's emotional design and designing for pleasure

topics.title: User Experience Questionnaire
topics.summary: UEQ is a structured way to measure dimensions of user experience rather than relying on vague impressions.
topics.fact: The lecture used the UEQ and its short version to compare apps.
topics.example: Rating systems with the short version of UEQ

topics.title: Iterative Human-Centered Design
topics.summary: Human-centered design proceeds through recurring activities rather than a single linear pass.
topics.fact: The model for HCD was shown as four activities with multiple iterations.
topics.example: Repeatedly revisiting research, design, prototyping, and evaluation

topics.title: Discovering the Problem Space
topics.summary: Design teams must understand the real problem before trying to build a solution, so they build the right thing rather than merely building correctly.
topics.fact: The lecture contrasted building the thing right with identifying the right thing to build.
topics.example: Windows CE versus iPhone as a problem-space lesson

topics.title: PACT Framework
topics.summary: PACT analyzes people, activities, context, and technology to structure design understanding.
topics.fact: PACT was introduced as a user-centric framework for thinking about design problems.
topics.example: Considering user differences, physical context, and task purpose together

topics.title: Perception and Visual Biases
topics.summary: Human perception is not a perfect recording system and can be biased or fooled by visual structure.
topics.fact: The lecture used visual illusions to show that perception can be biased.
topics.example: Size illusion demonstrations

topics.title: Gestalt Principles
topics.summary: Gestalt laws such as proximity, similarity, continuity, closure, symmetry, figure-ground, and common fate help explain how users group visual information.
topics.fact: Gestalt principles describe how vision is optimized for seeing structure.
topics.example: Grouping nearby items through proximity

topics.title: Visual Hierarchy and Structuring
topics.summary: Designers can guide interpretation by using size, location, chunking, and spacing to create a clear information hierarchy.
topics.fact: The lecture emphasized that users infer informational structure from visual hierarchy.
topics.example: Using spacing and chunking to surface important information

topics.title: Attention and Bandwidth Limits
topics.summary: Visual attention is limited, so clutter and weak hierarchy can hide important information or overload users.
topics.fact: The lecture explicitly states that vision has limited bandwidth.
topics.example: Subitizing and attention tasks that reveal perceptual limits

topics.title: Memory Systems
topics.summary: The lecture distinguishes sensory memory, working memory, and long-term memory as different parts of cognitive processing.
topics.fact: The dark-room analogy was used to explain sensory memory, and working memory was discussed as short-term and limited.
topics.example: Sensory memory, working memory, and long-term memory

topics.title: Recognition versus Recall
topics.summary: Recognition is usually easier than recall, so interfaces should provide cues instead of forcing users to remember everything unaided.
topics.fact: The lecture explicitly says recognition is easy and recall is hard.
topics.example: Choosing from visible options instead of remembering commands

topics.title: Cognitive Workload and Rehearsal
topics.summary: Interfaces can increase or reduce cognitive workload, and rehearsal helps users maintain or retrieve information.
topics.fact: The lecture connects rehearsal to preventing forgetting and treats cognitive workload as an important metric.
topics.example: Reducing memory burden with external cues

topics.title: Descriptive and Predictive Models
topics.summary: HCI uses descriptive and predictive models to explain human behavior, including bimanual coordination models such as Guiard's model.
topics.fact: Guiard's model was presented as a descriptive model of bimanual tasks.
topics.example: Analyzing two-handed interaction with Guiard's model

topics.title: Fitts' Law
topics.summary: Fitts' Law predicts pointing time from target distance and width through an index of difficulty.
topics.fact: The movement time equation uses target distance and width to model pointing performance.
topics.example: Larger and closer targets being faster to acquire

topics.title: Steering Law
topics.summary: Steering Law extends pointing models to tasks that require moving through a constrained path or tunnel.
topics.fact: The lecture describes Steering Law as mathematically derived from Fitts' Law.
topics.example: Navigating a cursor through a narrow menu tunnel

topics.title: Keystroke-Level Model
topics.summary: KLM estimates how long expert users take to perform routine tasks by breaking actions into low-level operators.
topics.fact: KLM was introduced as an instance of the Model Human Processor.
topics.example: Millisecond-level estimates of text entry or GUI tasks

topics.title: Power Law of Practice
topics.summary: The power law of practice predicts that repeated performance becomes faster with experience.
topics.fact: The lecture applied the power law to typing and other repeated motor tasks.
topics.example: Users getting faster with a new keyboard over repeated trials

topics.title: Computational UI Design
topics.summary: Computational design uses models and algorithms to evaluate or generate interface designs.
topics.fact: The lecture described many UI design tasks as optimization problems.
topics.example: Using algorithms to improve a menu layout

topics.title: Computational Design of Menus
topics.summary: Menu design can be evaluated and generated computationally based on predicted user performance.
topics.fact: The lecture asked both how good a menu is and where items should be placed.
topics.example: Assessing and generating menu structures automatically

topics.title: Purpose of User Research
topics.summary: User research aims to obtain concrete empirical knowledge about users and their tasks for design decisions.
topics.fact: The lecture positioned user research as part of understanding tasks in the human-centered design process.
topics.example: Identifying and validating tasks before designing features

topics.title: Interviews, Observation, and Contextual Inquiry
topics.summary: Different user research methods reveal different kinds of evidence, with contextual inquiry combining observation and interview in real context.
topics.fact: The lecture describes contextual inquiry as observation plus interview guided by four principles.
topics.example: Using observation to address the say-do gap

topics.title: Data Collection Tradeoffs
topics.summary: Researchers must balance realism, precision, and generalizability while also considering ethics and triangulation.
topics.fact: The lecture explicitly names realism, precision, and generalizability as key criteria.
topics.example: Triangulating interviews with questionnaires

topics.title: Quantitative and Qualitative Analysis
topics.summary: Quantitative analysis focuses on numbers and trends, while qualitative analysis focuses on text, images, video, and meaning.
topics.fact: The lecture separates quantitative and qualitative analysis by data type and purpose.
topics.example: Numbers and trends versus interview transcripts and images

topics.title: Affinity Diagrams and Coding Tools
topics.summary: Affinity diagrams and coding tools help organize, label, and interpret qualitative findings.
topics.fact: The lecture mentions tools such as Miro, Otter.ai, and MAXQDA.
topics.example: Grouping related interview observations on a Miro board

topics.title: Tasks, Personas, and Requirements
topics.summary: After research, teams create task descriptions, prioritize users and tasks, develop personas, and separate functional from non-functional requirements.
topics.fact: Good task descriptions focus on what the user wants to do rather than prescribing interface details.
topics.example: A task description that describes the complete job from start to finish

topics.title: Functional Requirements
topics.summary: Functional requirements describe what the system must do for users.
topics.fact: The lecture defines a requirement as a functionality or quality the product must have.
topics.example: A roommate-finding system matching compatible users

topics.title: Non-Functional Requirements
topics.summary: Non-functional requirements specify qualities such as usability, performance, or reliability rather than core features.
topics.fact: The lecture describes non-functional requirements as characteristics of the product.
topics.example: A system needing to be fast, secure, and easy to use

topics.title: Mental Models
topics.summary: Conceptual design tries to reduce mismatch among the user's mental model, the designer's model, and the implementation model.
topics.fact: The user's model was equated with the mental model in the lecture.
topics.example: Users misunderstanding how a fridge or freezer control works

topics.title: Conceptual Design and Sketching
topics.summary: Conceptual design clarifies how a system should work before detailed implementation, and sketches help explore ideas quickly.
topics.fact: The lecture highlights why conceptual design matters before committing to solutions.
topics.example: Sketching interaction ideas before building a detailed prototype

topics.title: Why Prototype
topics.summary: A prototype is a model of a product built to communicate, improve, or evaluate design ideas before final implementation.
topics.fact: The lecture defines prototypes as models built for communication, improvement, or evaluation.
topics.example: Using a rough model to test an interaction before coding

topics.title: Storyboards
topics.summary: Storyboards show how a user moves through a task over time and help teams reason about scenarios and context.
topics.fact: The lecture describes storyboards as a series of sketches showing task progression.
topics.example: Drawing frames of a user interacting with a kiosk

topics.title: Paper Prototyping
topics.summary: Paper prototypes simulate interface interaction with physical screens or widgets that can be changed quickly.
topics.fact: Paper prototyping was presented as a basic low-fidelity interaction simulation technique.
topics.example: Swapping paper screens to mimic navigation

topics.title: Horizontal and Vertical Prototypes
topics.summary: Horizontal prototypes explore breadth across features, while vertical prototypes explore one feature in depth.
topics.fact: The lecture asks students to describe the main purpose of horizontal and vertical prototypes.
topics.example: Broad coverage of many screens versus deep testing of one workflow

topics.title: Physical Prototyping
topics.summary: Physical prototypes are especially useful when the form, feel, or embodied interaction of a system matters.
topics.fact: The lecture explicitly says physical prototyping is best suited when physical form and feel matter.
topics.example: Mocking up a tangible object to test grip and manipulation

topics.title: Wizard of Oz Prototyping
topics.summary: Wizard of Oz prototyping lets a hidden human simulate system intelligence or automation during testing.
topics.fact: The lecture describes Wizard of Oz as a behavioral prototyping technique for technically complex systems.
topics.example: A person secretly acting as the speech recognizer

topics.title: Prototype Fidelity
topics.summary: Low, medium, and high fidelity prototypes support different stages and goals in the design process.
topics.fact: The lecture notes that low fidelity is common earlier and medium or high fidelity later.
topics.example: Paper sketches early versus polished interactive mockups later

topics.title: Range of Evaluation Methods
topics.summary: Usability testing methods vary in realism, control, cost, and what they reveal, from informal tests to controlled experiments and A/B testing.
topics.fact: The lecture introduces usability testing methods and basics of controlled experiments and A/B testing.
topics.example: Comparing two interface variants with users

topics.title: Controlled Experiment Components
topics.summary: Controlled experiments define questions, hypotheses, variables, conditions, measurements, and possible confounds.
topics.fact: The lecture lists conditions, dependent variables, and influencing factors as key components.
topics.example: Comparing interface A versus B using completion time and error rate

topics.title: Study Design Choices
topics.summary: Study design choices such as between-subjects structure affect learning effects, participant workload, and validity.
topics.fact: The lecture explicitly discusses between-subjects design and its pros and cons.
topics.example: Each participant seeing only one interface condition

topics.title: Hypothesis Testing
topics.summary: Statistical hypothesis testing helps determine whether observed differences are likely due to chance.
topics.fact: The lecture describes hypothesis testing as using established statistical procedures to reject or fail to reject hypotheses.
topics.example: Testing whether two interface samples likely come from the same distribution

topics.title: Data Types and Statistical Tests
topics.summary: Choosing a statistical test depends on the type of data and the study design.
topics.fact: The lecture links categorical and numerical data types to different analysis choices such as the independent-samples t-test.
topics.example: Using a two independent sample t-test for two groups

topics.title: Participant-Based versus Expert-Based Evaluation
topics.summary: Evaluation methods can either directly involve users or rely on expert inspection without users present.
topics.fact: The lecture contrasts participant-based empirical methods with expert-based inspection methods.
topics.example: User testing versus heuristic evaluation

topics.title: Cognitive Walkthrough
topics.summary: A cognitive walkthrough evaluates how easily a user can discover and execute a task sequence step by step.
topics.fact: The lecture includes preparation and analysis phases for walkthroughs.
topics.example: Checking whether users know what to do at each action

topics.title: Heuristic Evaluation
topics.summary: Heuristic evaluation inspects an interface against established usability rules of thumb.
topics.fact: The lecture defines a heuristic as a rule of thumb.
topics.example: Inspecting a website against Nielsen's heuristics

topics.title: Nielsen's Heuristics
topics.summary: Nielsen's heuristics provide a structured lens for spotting usability problems such as poor visibility or mismatch with the real world.
topics.fact: The lecture presents the 10 usability heuristics from Nielsen.
topics.example: Visibility of system status

topics.title: Accessibility as Barrier Removal
topics.summary: Accessibility focuses on removing barriers that would otherwise exclude people from using a system at all.
topics.fact: The lecture quotes accessibility as removing barriers that would exclude some people from use.
topics.example: Design changes that let more people access the system

topics.title: Types of Accessibility Barriers
topics.summary: Barriers can be physical, conceptual, economic, and tied to mismatches between user needs and system assumptions.
topics.fact: The lecture explicitly categorizes several kinds of barriers rather than limiting accessibility to one issue.
topics.example: A conceptual barrier caused by confusing instructions

topics.title: Situational Impairment
topics.summary: People can experience accessibility challenges temporarily or contextually even without a permanent disability.
topics.fact: The lecture states that disability or impairment can be situational.
topics.example: Using a system in glare, noise, or with one hand occupied

topics.title: Curb Cut Effect and Inclusive Design
topics.summary: Designing for accessibility often produces broader benefits for many users, not just the initially targeted group.
topics.fact: The lecture uses the curb cut effect to show how inclusive design helps wider populations.
topics.example: Curb cuts helping strollers and deliveries as well as wheelchairs

topics.title: Value of Social Computing
topics.summary: Social computing adds a social layer to interaction and supports communication, collaboration, and broader participation.
topics.fact: The lecture describes social computing as computing with significant social interaction.
topics.example: Systems that support collaboration beyond an individual user

topics.title: Groupware Time-Space Matrix
topics.summary: Groupware can be classified by whether collaboration happens in the same or different place and at the same or different time.
topics.fact: The lecture uses time and space to classify groupware technologies.
topics.example: Video conferencing as different place, same time

topics.title: Awareness, Tailoring, and Failure Modes
topics.summary: Collaborative systems need awareness and tailoring, and they can fail when work and benefit are misaligned or evaluation is difficult.
topics.fact: The lecture names awareness and tailoring as key considerations for groupware.
topics.example: A system failing because contributors do work without seeing enough benefit

topics.title: Crowdsourcing and Data Quality
topics.summary: Crowdsourcing opens work to large groups, but task structure and quality controls strongly influence result validity.
topics.fact: The lecture discusses random answers and quality controls in crowdsourced work.
topics.example: Embedding quality checks in a crowd task

topics.title: HAI and Historical Framing
topics.summary: Human-AI interaction sits at the intersection of HCI and AI and has roots in debates about intelligence augmentation and interface agents.
topics.fact: The lecture cites the divide between AI and HCI while keeping a common focus on human work.
topics.example: Direct manipulation versus interface agents

topics.title: Mixed-Initiative Design
topics.summary: Mixed-initiative systems let humans and AI agents share initiative rather than giving all control to one side.
topics.fact: The lecture defines mixed-initiative interfaces as ones where users and intelligent agents collaborate.
topics.example: Email scheduling assistance in LookOut

topics.title: Action, Dialog, and Timing
topics.summary: Designers must decide when AI should act automatically, when it should ask, and how timing affects user trust and burden.
topics.fact: The lecture discusses when to engage in dialog to resolve uncertainties.
topics.example: A calendar pop-up that asks at the right moment

topics.title: Augment versus Automate
topics.summary: Some tasks are better supported through augmentation while others can be automated, depending on uncertainty, stakes, and user goals.
topics.fact: The lecture explicitly asks students to choose whether AI should automate or augment tasks such as spam filtering.
topics.example: Filtering spam emails

topics.title: Design Challenges for AI Systems
topics.summary: AI-powered systems are hard to design because of limited AI literacy, prototyping difficulty, error communication challenges, and coordination with engineers.
topics.fact: The lecture lists lack of AI knowledge and difficulty of prototyping for AI among the challenges.
topics.example: Communicating AI errors and impact to users

topics.title: AI Design Complexity Map
topics.summary: AI systems vary in output complexity and how much they evolve over time, which affects design difficulty.
topics.fact: The lecture contrasts limited fixed-output systems with evolving adaptive systems.
topics.example: Level 1 probabilistic systems versus Level 4 evolving adaptive systems

topics.title: Human-AI Interaction Guidelines
topics.summary: Guidelines for HAI cover setting expectations, taking context into account during use, supporting recovery when wrong, and handling feedback over time.
topics.fact: The lecture uses Microsoft's guideline groups such as initially, during interaction, when wrong, and over time.
topics.example: Setting user expectations before the system acts

topics.title: Characteristics of HAI Collaboration
topics.summary: Good collaboration depends on complementary performance, alignment, cooperative interaction, reasonable task allocation, and appropriate trust.
topics.fact: The lecture lists five desirable characteristics for human-AI collaboration.
topics.example: Complementary performance between human and AI strengths

topics.title: Appropriate Trust in HAI
topics.summary: People should rely on AI when it is correct but remain able to notice and override mistakes.
topics.fact: The lecture discusses appropriate trust and factors that cause inappropriate reliance.
topics.example: Accepting AI recommendations only when they make sense

topics.title: WIMP and Direct Manipulation
topics.summary: Classic GUIs use windows, icons, menus, and pointers and are closely tied to direct manipulation interaction.
topics.fact: The lecture uses WIMP to explain the structure of traditional graphical user interfaces.
topics.example: Windows, icons, menus, and pointers

topics.title: Post-WIMP and Ubiquitous Computing
topics.summary: Post-WIMP interaction expands beyond traditional widgets into richer modalities and more embedded forms of computing.
topics.fact: The lecture describes post-WIMP interfaces as building on pre-existing knowledge but introducing many new modalities.
topics.example: Touch, body input, and other non-WIMP modalities

topics.title: XR Terminology and VR Basics
topics.summary: The lecture distinguishes VR, AR, MR, and XR and defines VR around strong illusions of presence and interaction.
topics.fact: A chief goal of VR was described as creating a strong illusion for users.
topics.example: VR, AR, MR, and XR terminology

topics.title: Locomotion in VR
topics.summary: VR locomotion concerns how users move through virtual environments despite physical-world limits.
topics.fact: The lecture lists room-scale, redirected walking, joystick movement, teleportation, gesture, and specialized hardware schemes.
topics.example: Teleportation as a locomotion technique

topics.title: Reality-Based, Illusory, and Magical Interaction
topics.summary: VR techniques can mimic real-world actions, exploit perceptual illusions, or give users superhuman capabilities.
topics.fact: The lecture explicitly distinguishes reality-based, illusory, and beyond-real interactions.
topics.example: Scaling space or user movement for magical interaction

topics.title: VR Sickness
topics.summary: VR sickness can result from conflicts such as vergence-accommodation mismatch or visual-vestibular mismatch.
topics.fact: The lecture names vergence-accommodation conflict and visual-vestibular mismatch as causes.
topics.example: Motion discomfort caused by conflicting sensory cues

topics.title: Object Selection and Manipulation in VR
topics.summary: VR interaction design includes choosing techniques for nearby and distant object selection and manipulation.
topics.fact: The lecture covers ray casting, Go-Go, Ninja Hands, World in Miniature, and haptic retargeting.
topics.example: Ray casting for far object selection

topics.title: XR Accessibility
topics.summary: Accessible XR design must consider content accessibility, input accessibility, and inclusive representation.
topics.fact: The lecture notes that accessibility of VR is still in its infancy.
topics.example: Accessible input techniques and inclusive avatars

topics.title: Touchscreen Input
topics.summary: Touchscreens support direct hand-based interaction but introduce challenges such as reduced tactile cues and execution difficulty.
topics.fact: The lecture contrasts resistive sensing, multi-touch, and touchscreen challenges.
topics.example: Multi-touch sensing and the gulf of execution

topics.title: Gesture Interfaces
topics.summary: Gesture-based systems rely on sensing and interpreting hand or body movements, but they face sensing and design challenges.
topics.fact: The lecture names lack of a universal gesture language as an engineering challenge.
topics.example: Wearable sensors for hand-gesture sensing

topics.title: Hand and Full-Body Tracking
topics.summary: Tracking continuous motion requires mapping sensed movement into accurate and efficient interaction techniques.
topics.fact: The lecture distinguishes gesture recognition from continuous hand tracking and full-body tracking.
topics.example: Improving accuracy and efficiency in hand tracking

topics.title: Speech-Based Interfaces
topics.summary: Speech interfaces can be efficient and natural, but they are sequential and raise recognition and feedback challenges.
topics.fact: The lecture mentions directed dialog, high speaking rate, and the problem of voice-mail-like deep hierarchies.
topics.example: Directed-dialog systems controlling the conversation

topics.title: Gaze-Based Interaction
topics.summary: Gaze can indicate attention and support selection, but it is often combined with another modality to avoid errors.
topics.fact: The lecture explicitly points out that gaze-based interaction can require training and be error-prone.
topics.example: Combining gaze with another input modality for selection

topics.title: Haptic Sense and Exploration
topics.summary: Haptic perception is distributed across the body and depends on active exploratory procedures rather than passive contact alone.
topics.fact: The lecture introduces exploratory procedures and tactile stereognosis.
topics.example: Using hand movements to infer texture or shape

topics.title: Receptors and Haptic Illusions
topics.summary: Different receptors and illusions help explain how tactile and kinesthetic haptic perception can be designed or manipulated.
topics.fact: The lecture covers mechanoreceptors, C-tactile afferents, and tactile apparent motion illusion.
topics.example: Creating motion sensation from two actuators

topics.title: Subareas of Haptics
topics.summary: Haptics spans human, machine, computer, and interaction haptics as an interdisciplinary field.
topics.fact: The lecture repeatedly shows haptics as an interdisciplinary field with multiple subareas.
topics.example: Human, machine, computer, and interaction haptics

topics.title: Haptic Illusions
topics.summary: Illusions such as the rubber hand illusion and tactile apparent motion reveal how haptic perception can be shaped.
topics.fact: The lecture covers rubber hand illusion and apparent motion illusion in tactile perception.
topics.example: Rubber hand illusion

topics.title: Machine Haptics and Device Types
topics.summary: Machine haptics focuses on building touch technologies including grounded, wearable, cable-driven, EMS, and pin-array devices.
topics.fact: The lecture surveys several device classes and asks whether examples are tactile or kinesthetic.
topics.example: PhanToM Omni force-feedback device

topics.title: Computer Haptics and Rendering
topics.summary: Computer haptics studies how to render haptic stimuli and simulate contact such as rigid surfaces.
topics.fact: The lecture includes rendering a wall or rigid surface as a computer haptics example.
topics.example: Rendering a rigid wall in a haptic system

topics.title: Applications of Haptics
topics.summary: Haptics is used in domains such as surgery, prosthetics, VR, MR, accessibility, education, and teleoperation.
topics.fact: The lecture gives example applications including robot-assisted surgery and physical feedback for prosthetics.
topics.example: Robot-assisted surgery

topics.title: Role of Haptics in Multisensory Interaction
topics.summary: Haptic feedback can complement or reinforce information from vision, audio, and other modalities.
topics.fact: The lecture asks students to identify when haptics plays complementary or reinforcing roles.
topics.example: Complementary versus reinforcing haptic feedback

topics.title: Haptic Interaction Design Process
topics.summary: Designing haptic interactions involves browsing, sketching, refining, and sharing or evaluating ideas.
topics.fact: The lecture presents a four-activity design process for haptics.
topics.example: Browsing with Haptipedia and sketching vibrotactile stimuli

topics.title: Generating and Evaluating Haptics
topics.summary: Modern work generates haptics from text, video, audio, VR scenes, or AI methods and evaluates signals with human or AI-assisted approaches.
topics.fact: The lecture includes text-to-vibration, sound-to-vibration, video-based generation, and human-based versus AI-powered evaluation.
topics.example: Sound2Hap audio-to-vibration generation
